title: 第 52 節
en: '0 Bharata1, what need has a self-controlled man of the forest, and what use is the forest to an uncon trolled man '{ Wherever a self-controlled man dwells, that is a forest, that is an hermitage.
'A sage, even though he remains in his house, dressed in fine apparel, if only always pure, and full of love, as long as life lasts, becomes freed from all evils2.
1 jSantiparva, 5961, dantasya kimaranvena tatMdantasj^a bharata yatraiva nivased dantas tadarawyam sa kasramah.
2 Vanaparva, 13450, tish<7*an grille &aiva munir cityam sukir alahkrita/t yava^ivam dayavams ka sarvapapai/t pramu&yate.

PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 355 ' Carrying the three staves, observing silence, wearing platted hair, shaving the head, clothing oneself in dresses of bark or skins, performing vows and ablution, the agnihotra-sacrifice, dwelling in the forest, and emaciating the body, all these are vain, if the heart is not pure1.'

Such ideas become in time more and more pre valent, and contributed 110 doubt to the victory of Buddhism, in which all external works and marks had ceased to be considered as of any value. Thus we read in the Buddhist aphorisms of the Dhammapada2, Nos. 141, 142 :
'Not nakedness, not platted hair, not dirt, not fasting, or lying on the earth, not rubbing with dust, nor sitting motionless, can purify a mortal who has not overcome desires.

' He who, though dressed in fine apparel, exer cises tranquillity, is quiet, subdued, restrained, chaste, and has ceased to find fault with all other beings 3, he indeed is a Brahma?ia, a $rama?m (ascetic), a Bhikshu (a friar).'

All these thoughts had passed again and again through the minds of Indian thinkers as they pass through our own, and had received simple and beautiful expression in their religious and epic poetry. I need only mention here from the Maha- 1 Vanaparva, 13445, trida«cZadhara^am maunam ^raiabharo 'tha muM<2anam, valkal%unasamveshtom vrata&aryabhishe&anam, agnihotram vanevasa/i sariraparisoshawara, sarvany etani mithya syur yadi bhavo na nirmalaA.
2 Buddhaghosha's 'Parables,' ed. M. M., 1870, p. xcviii.
3 dawcZanidhana is explained by vahmana/tkayair himsatyagaA, in the commentary on the Mahabharata, /Santiparva, 175? v- 37- A a 2 356 LECTURE VII.

bharata 1 the curious dialogue between king (kanaka and Sulabha, who, in the guise of a beautiful woman, convicts him of deceiving himself in imagining that he can be at the same time a king and a sage, living in the world, yet being not of the world. This is the same king kanaka of Videha who gloried in saying that if his capital Mithila were in flames, nothing belonging to him would be burnt 2.

Still the ancient Brahmans retained their conviction that, after the first and second stages of life were passed, when a man was fifty — what we in our in satiable love of work call the very best yearsof a man's life — he had a right to rest, to look inward and backward and forward, before it was too late.

It would be out of place here to enter into any his torical disquisitions as to the advantages of these two systems in retarding or accelerating the real progress, the real civilization, and the attainment of the real objects of human life. Only let us not, as we are so apt to do, condemn what seems strange to us, or exalt what seems familiar. Our senators and elders have, no doubt, rendered important services ; but their authority and influence have many a time been used in history to check and chill the liberal and generous tendencies of younger hearts. It may be a true saying that young men imagine that old men are fools, and that old men know that young men are ; but is it not equally true of many a man eminent in Church and State, that, in exact pro portion as the vigour of his mind and the freshness 1 Mahabharata, £antiparva, Adhyaya 320; ed. Bombay, vol. v, p. 227 seq. Muir, 'Religious and Moral Sentiments,' p. 126.
2 Dharmapada, translated by M. M., p. cxv.

PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 357 of his sentiments decrease, his authority and in fluence increase for evil rather than for good \ And remember, this life in the forest was not an involuntary exile ; it was looked upon as a privilege, and no one was admitted to it who had not conscien tiously fulfilled all the duties of the student and the householder. That previous discipline was considered essential to subdue the unruly passions of the human heart. During that period of probation and prepa ration, that is, during the best part of a man's life, little freedom was allowed in thought or deed. As the student had been taught, so he had to believe, so he had to pray, so he had to sacrifice to the gods. The Vedas were his sacred books, and their claims to a supernatural origin, to be considered as revelation, were more carefully and minutely guarded in the apologetic literature of India than in any other theological literature which I know.

And yet, on a sudden, as soon as a man entered upon the third stage or the forest-life, he was eman cipated from all these fetters. He might carry on some outward observances for a time, he might say his prayers, he might repeat the scriptures which he had acquired as a boy, but his chief object was to concentrate his thoughts on the eternal Self, such as it was revealed in the Upanishads. The more he found his true home there, and could give up all that he had formerly called his own, divesting himself of his Ego, and all that was personal and transient, and recovering his true Self in the eternal Self, the more all fetters of law, of custom, and caste, of tradition and outward religion fell from him. The Vedas now became to him the lower knowledge only ; the sacrifices were looked 358 LECTURE VII.

upon as hindrances ; the old gods, Agni and Indra, Mitra and Varima, Visvakarman also and Pragrapati, all vanished as mere names. There remained only the Atman, the subjective, and Brahman, the objective Self, and the highest knowledge was expressed in the words tat tvam, thou art it ; thou thyself, thy own true Self, that which can never be taken from thee, when everything else that seemed to be thine for a time, disappears ; when all that was created vanishes again like a dream, thy own true Self belongs to the eternal Self; the Atrnan or Self within thee is the true Brah man \ from whom thou wast estranged for a time 1 I have avoided to use the word Brahman instead of Atman, because, though its later development is clear, I must confess that I have not been able as yet to gain a clear conception of its real roots. As for all other abstract conceptions, there must be for brahman also something tangible from which it sprang, but what this was, seems to me still very doubtful.

There can be little doubt that the root from which brahman was derived is brth or vrz'h. The meanings ascribed to this root by native grammarians are to erect, to strive, and to grow. These three meanings may be reduced to one, viz. to push, which, if used intransitively, would mean to spring up, to grow ; if transitively, to make spring up, to erect.

Between these meanings, however, and the meanings assigned to Brahman by the oldest exegetes, there seems little connection. Yaska explains brahman as meaning either food or wealth. Sayana adopts these meanings and adds to them some others, such as hymn, hymn of praise, sacrifice, also great (brihat). (See Haug, Uber die urspriinglich Bedeutung des Wortes Brahma, 1868, p. 4.) Professor Roth gives as the first meaning of brah man, (i) pious meditation appearing as an impulse and fulness of the mind, and striving towards the gods, every pious manifestation at divine service, (2) sacred formula, (3) sacred word, word of God, (4) sacred wisdom, theology, theosophy, (5) sacred life, chastity, (6) the highest object of theosophy, the impersonal god, the PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 359 through birth and death, but who receives thee back again as soon as thou returnest to Him, or to It.

absolute, (7) the clergy. Professor Haug, on the contrary, thinks that brahman meant originally a small broom made of Kusa grass, which during a sacrifice is handed round, and is also called veda, i. e. tied together, a bundle. He identifies it, as Benfey before him, with the Zend baresman, always used at the Izeshne ceremony, which is a reflex of the Vedic Soma-saci-ifice. The original meaning of brahman and baresman he supposes to have been sprouts or shoots (Lat. virga). then growth, prosperity. As the prosperity of a sacrifice depended on the hymns and prayers, these too were called brahman, the sacrifice was called brahman, and at last this prosperity was conceived as the first cause of all being.

Neither of these biographies seems to me altogether satisfactory. Without attempting to explain here my own view of the origin and growth of brahman, I shall only say that there is a third meaning assigned to the root brih, to sound or to speak. Speech, in its most general meaning, may have been conceived as what springs forth and grows, then also as what not only develops itself, but develops its objects also, more particularly the gods, who are named and praised in words. From the root vrih, determined in that direction, we have, I believe, the Latin verb-um, and the Gothic vaurd, word (cf. barba and 0. N. bar%-r, urbs and Sanskrit ardha, etc. ; Ascoli in Kuhn's Zeitschrift, XVII, 334). How far the Indians retained the consciousness of the original meaning of brih and brahma, is difficult to say, but it is curious to see how they use Bn'has-pati and Va&as-pati, as synonymes of the same deity. In the Brthadai^myaka, I, 3, 20, we read : esha u eva brihaspatir, vag vai brihati, tasya esha patis, tasmad u bn'haspatiA ; esha u eva brahmawaspatir, vag vai brahma, tasya esha patis, tasmad u brahmawaspati7i. Here the identity of vak, speech, with brihati (or brih) and brahman is clearly asserted. From the root vrih, in the sense of growing, we have in Sanskrit barhis, shoots, grass, bundle of grass, in Latin virga. The Latin verbenae, also, the sacred branches, borne by the fetiales, and possibly the verbera (verberibus caedere), may come from the same root. Without attempting to trace the further ramifications of brahman, word, hymn of praise, prayer, sacrifice, I shall only guard at once against 360 LECTURE VII.
The end.

Here is the end of the long journey which we undertook to trace ; here the infinite, which had been seen as behind a veil in the mountains and rivers, in the sun and the sky, in the endless dawn, in the heavenly father, in Visvakarman, the maker of all things, in Pra^/apati, the lord of all living creatures, was seen at last in the highest and purest form which the Indian intellect could reach. Can we define him, they said, or comprehend him ? No, they replied ; all we can say of him, is No, no ! He is not this. He is not that ; he is not the maker, not the father, not the sky or the sun, not the rivers or the mountains. Whatever we have called him, that he is not. We cannot comprehend or name him, but we can feel him ; we cannot know him, but we can apprehend him ; and if we have once found him, we can never escape from him. We are at rest, we are free, we are blessed. They waited patiently for the few years before death would release them : they did nothing to prolong their old age, but at the same time they thought it wrong to put an end to their life them selves1. They had reached what was to them eternal life on earth, and they felt convinced that no new birth and death could separate them again from that the idea that we have in it some kind of Logos. Though brahman comes in the end to mean the cause of the universe, and is fre quently identified with the highest Atman or Self, its development was different from that of the Alexandrian Logos, and historically, at all events, these two streams of thought are entirely uncon nected.
1 Manu, VI, 45, ' Let him not wish for death, let him not wish for life ; let him expect the appointed time, as an hired servant expects his wages.'
PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 361 eternal Self which they had found, or which had found them.

And yet they did not believe in the annihilation of their own Self. Eemember the dialogue in which Indra was introduced as patiently acquiring a know ledge of the Self. He first looks for the Self in the shadow in the water ; then in the soul while dream ing ; then in the soul when in deepest sleep. But he is dissatisfied even then, and says : ' No, this cannot be ; for he, the sleeper, does not know himself (his self) that he is I, nor does he know anything that exists. He is gone to utter annihilation. I see no good in this.'

But what does his teacher reply ? ' This body is mortal/ he says, ' and always held by death, but it is the abode of the Self, which is immortal and without a body. When embodied (when thinking this body is I, and I am this body) the Self is held by pleasure and pain. So long as he is thus embodied, he cannot get rid of pleasure and pain. But when the Self is disembodied (when he knows himself to be different from the body), then neither pleasure nor pain can touch him any more.

Yet this Self, the serene soul, or the highest person, does not perish, it only comes to himself again ; it rejoices even, it laughs and plays, but as a spectator only, never remembering the body of his birth. He is the Self of the eye, the eye itself is but an instrument : He who knows I will say this, I will hear this, I will think this, he is the Self; the tongue, the ear, the mind are but instruments. The mind is his divine eye, and through that divine eye the Self sees all that is beautiful, and rejoices.
Here we see that annihilation was certainly not the 362 LECTURE VII.
zh: > 0 婆羅多¹，一個自制的人何需森林？而一個不自制的人，森林又有何用？¹ > 無論自制之人居於何處，那便是森林，那便是隱修所。
> 「一位智者，即使身居家中、衣著華美，只要始終純淨、充滿慈愛，盡其一生，便能脫離一切罪惡²。」

> *「若人已得自制，何需林居？* > *婆羅多啊！自制者所居之處，即是森林，即是苦行地。」* > ——《摩訶婆羅多‧和平篇》（Śāntiparva, 5961） 在宗教學（the science of religion）的領域中，我們首先必須釐清其與比較神學（comparative theology）及比較神話學（comparative mythology）之間的界線。語言科學（the science of language）的發展已為我們揭示，雅利安（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）諸民族的神話體系，並非全然出於偶然的幻想，而是反映了人類心智對無限者（the Infinite）的原始直觀。穆勒（Müller）指出，古代吠陀（Veda）文獻，尤其是《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda），展現了從多神教（polytheism）過渡到單一神教（henotheism）乃至輪換主神教（kathenotheism）的歷程——在這種信仰型態中，每一位被呼喚的神祇，如特尤斯（Dyaus）或提婆（Deva），在當下都被視為至高無上的存在。 然而，穆勒警告我們，神話的起源往往源於「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）：當原本描述自然現象的詞彙失去其原始意義，後人便以字面方式理解這些隱喻，從而產生了看似荒誕不經的神話敘事。這種現象在《奧義書》（Upanishad）的哲學思辨中尤為明顯——智者們試圖穿透語言的迷障，直探那超越一切名相的終極真實。
> 2 Vanaparva, 13450, tish<7*an grille &aiva munir cityam sukir alahkrita/t yava^ivam dayavams ka sarvapapai/t pramu&yate.
「手持三杖、保持沉默、編結髮辮、剃除頭頂、身披樹皮或獸皮、履行誓願與沐浴、行阿耆尼火祭（agnihotra-sacrifice）、居於林中、消瘦其身——若心不純淨，這一切皆屬虛妄¹。」
隨著時間推移，這類觀念變得愈發盛行，無疑促成了佛教的勝利——在佛教中，一切外在的業行與表徵已不再被視為具有任何價值。因此，我們在《法句經》（Dhammapada）的佛教偈頌第141、142則中讀到：
『非裸身、非結辮、非汙垢、非禁食、或臥於地、非塗塵、亦非靜坐不動，能淨化一位尚未克服慾望的凡人。』
> 「身穿華服，卻修習寂靜、平和、調伏、自制、貞潔，且已停止指責一切眾生者，他確實是婆羅門、沙門（苦行者）、比丘（託缽僧）。」

所有這些思緒，曾一再反覆掠過印度思想家的心頭，正如它們掠過我們自己的心頭一般，並在他們的宗教與史詩詩歌中，獲得了簡潔而優美的表達。我在此僅需提及《摩訶婆羅多‧森林篇》（Mahā-Vanaparva, 13445）中的一段：「tridaśadhārāṇāṃ maunaṃ śraībharo 'tha muṇḍānām, valkalānasamveṣṭoṃ vrataṃ āryābhiṣekaṇam, agnihotraṃ vanevāsaḥ śarīraparisoṣaṇam, sarvāṇy etāni mithyā syur yadi bhāvo na nirmalaḥ。」（若心念不純淨，則持守天界之流、緘默、頂戴吉祥負荷、剃度、樹皮衣、裸身、誓戒、聖禮、祭火、林居、乃至苦行消瘦，這一切皆為虛妄。）
2 佛陀瞿沙（Buddhaghosha）的《譬喻經》（Parables），穆勒編，1870 年，第 xcviii 頁。
在《摩訶婆羅多》註釋（《和平篇》175？v-37）中，dawcZanidhana 被解釋為 vahmana/tkayair himsatyagaA。
bharata¹ 國王迦那迦（kanaka）與蘇拉巴（Sulabha）之間有一段奇特的對話：後者化身為一名美麗女子，指責前者自欺欺人，竟幻想自己能同時身為國王與聖賢，既活在世間，卻又不屬於世間。這位正是維德哈（Videha）的國王迦那迦，他曾自豪地宣稱，即便其都城彌提羅（Mithila）陷入火海，屬於他的一切也不會被燒毀²。
古代的婆羅門依然堅信，在人生的第一與第二階段度過之後——當一個人年屆五十，也就是我們因對工作貪得無厭而稱之為人生「最精華的歲月」——他有權休息，在為時未晚之前，向內省視、回顧過去並展望未來。

在此不宜深入探討這兩種體系在延緩或加速人類真正的進步、真正的文明以及達成人生真正目標方面有何歷史優劣。只是我們切勿像慣常所做的那樣，譴責那些對我們而言顯得陌生的事物，或推崇那些看似熟悉的事物。我們的元老與長者無疑曾立下重要功績；但在歷史上，他們的權威與影響力屢屢被用來壓制與冷卻年輕心靈中自由寬厚的傾向。有句老話或許說得對：年輕人以為老年人是傻瓜，而老年人知道年輕人確實是傻瓜；但對於許多在教會與國家中顯赫一時的人物而言，難道不也同樣真實——隨著他心智的活力與新鮮感¹ 精確地成比例遞減，他對新思想的包容度也隨之遞減？ ¹《摩訶婆羅多》‧〈和平篇〉，第320章；孟買版，第五卷，第227頁以下。繆爾，《宗教與道德情感》，第126頁。
2 法句經（Dharmapada），穆勒（M. M.）譯，第 cxv 頁。

他的情感影響力減弱，其權威與影響力便趨向邪惡而非良善。請記住，這種森林生活並非被迫流放；它被視為一種特權，唯有忠實履行學生與家主一切義務者，方能獲準進入。此前的規訓被視為剋制人類心中桀驁不馴之情的必要條件。在那段考驗與準備時期，亦即人生最精華的階段，無論思想或行為，幾乎不允許自由。學生如何被教導，就必須如何信仰、如何祈禱、如何向諸神獻祭。吠陀經是他們的聖典，而印度護教文獻對其超自然起源、視其為天啟的主張，其捍衛之審慎與細密，超越我所知的任何其他神學文獻。

然而，一旦人進入第三階段，即林棲期，他便驟然從所有這些束縛中獲得解放。他或許會暫時維持某些外在儀式，或許會誦唸祈禱文，或許會重複兒時習得的經文，但其首要目標乃是將思緒專注於永恆之我——即奧義書所啟示的那個自我。他愈是在其中尋得真正的歸宿，愈能捨棄昔日視為己有的一切，剝離自我（Ego）以及所有個人性與短暫性的事物，並在永恆之我中恢復其真實自我，那麼法律、習俗、種姓、傳統與外在宗教的種種束縛便愈發從他身上脫落。此時，吠陀對他而言僅屬低等知識；祭祀亦淪為……（第358頁，第七講）

被視為障礙；古老的神祇——阿耆尼（Agni）與因陀羅（Indra）、密多羅（Mitra）與伐樓那（Varuna），以及毘首羯磨（Viśvakarman）與生主（Prajāpati）——全都化為虛名而消逝。僅存的唯有阿特曼（Ātman），即主體自我，以及梵（Brahman），即客體自我；而最高的知識則表述於「彼即汝」（tat tvam asi）這句話中：你自身，你真正的自我，那永不會被奪走之物——當一切看似暫時屬於你的事物消失時，當所有受造之物如夢境般消散時，你真正的自我便歸屬於永恆的自我；你內在的阿特曼或自我，便是真正的梵（Brahman），你曾暫時與之疏離。我刻意避免使用「梵」來取代「阿特曼」，因為儘管其後期發展脈絡清晰，我必須承認，至今仍未能清楚掌握其真正的根源。如同所有其他抽象概念，「梵」也必然有其可觸及的源頭，但這個源頭究竟為何，在我看來仍十分可疑。
幾乎無可懷疑，梵語 *brahman* 所從出的詞根是 *brth* 或 *vrz'h*。本土文法學家歸於此詞根的意義為：豎立、奮力、生長。這三種意義可歸結為一種，即「推」（to push）；若作不及物動詞用，則意為「萌發」、「生長」；若作及物動詞用，則意為「使萌發」、「豎立」。

然而，這些意義與最古老的註釋家賦予「梵」（Brahman）的意義之間，似乎並無太多關聯。耶斯卡（Yaska）將「梵」解釋為食物或財富。薩亞納（Sayana）沿用這些解釋，並增添其他意義，如讚美詩、頌讚之詩、祭祀，以及「偉大」（brihat）。（參見豪格〔Haug〕，《論梵語詞「Brahma」的原始意義》〔Uber die ursprüngliche Bedeutung des Wortes Brahma〕，1868年，第4頁。）羅特教授（Professor Roth）則將「梵」的首要意義列為：(1) 虔敬的冥想，表現為心靈的衝動與充盈，並趨向諸神，即神聖崇拜中的每一種虔敬表現；(2) 神聖咒語；(3) 神聖之言、上帝之言；(4) 神聖智慧、神學、神智學；(5) 神聖生活、貞潔；(6) 神智學的最高對象——非位格的上帝，即**哲學與宗教**。第359頁：……歷經生死，但只要你回歸於祂（或它），祂便再次接納你。

> 絕對者，(7) 神職人員。反之，豪格教授認為，brahman 原指一種由古薩草製成的小掃帚，在祭祀時傳遞，亦稱為 veda，意即「捆紮在一起」、一束。他如同其前的本費，將此與《阿維斯陀》中的 baresman 視為同一物，該物始終用於伊澤什內儀式，此儀式為吠陀蘇摩祭之反映。他推測 brahman 與 baresman 的原義為「嫩芽或新枝」（拉丁文 virga），進而引申為「生長、繁榮」。由於祭祀的繁榮有賴於讚歌與祈禱，故這些亦被稱為 brahman，祭祀本身亦稱為 brahman，最終此種繁榮被構想為一切存有之第一因。

我認為這兩種解釋都不完全令人滿意。在此無意詳述我個人對 *brahman*（梵）起源與發展的看法，僅指出賦予詞根 *brih* 的第三種意義：發出聲響或說話。言語，就其最廣泛的意義而言，可能被理解為「迸發而出且生長」之物，進而也指涉不僅自我發展，同時也發展其對象——尤其是那些在言詞中被稱名與讚頌的神祇——之物。我相信，從朝此方向確定的詞根 *vrih*，我們得到了拉丁文的 *verb-um*（言詞）與哥德文的 *vaurd*（詞語）（參見 *barba* 與古北歐語 *barð-r*、*urbs* 與梵文 *ardha* 等；阿斯科利，見庫恩《期刊》第十七卷，第334頁）。印度人對 *brih* 與 *brahma* 原始意義的意識保留到何種程度，實難斷言；但觀察他們如何將 *Bṛhas-pati*（祈禱主）與 *Vācas-pati*（語言主）用作同一神祇的同義詞，則饒富趣味。在《百道梵書》I, 3, 20 中我們讀到：「此即 *brihaspati*（祈禱主）；*vāc*（言語）確為 *brihati*（廣大者），祂是此（言語）之主，故為 *brihaspati*；此即 *brahmaṇaspati*（梵主）；*vāc*（言語）確為 *brahman*（梵），祂是此（言語）之主，故為 *brahmaṇaspati*。」此處明確斷言 *vāk*（言語）與 *brihati*（或 *brih*）及 *brahman* 的同一性。從意為「生長」的詞根 *vrih*，我們在梵文中得到 *barhis*（草束、草墊、草捆），在拉丁文中得到 *virga*（嫩枝）。拉丁文的 *verbenae*（聖枝）——由 *fetiales*（祭司團）所持的神聖枝條——以及可能還有 *verbera*（鞭打，如 *verberibus caedere* 鞭笞），皆可能源出同一詞根。在此無意追溯 *brahman*（梵）——即言詞、讚美詩、祈禱、祭祀——的進一步衍伸，僅先立即防範……

因此，宗教學的目標，如同語言科學的目標一樣，在於發現人類心智在宗教領域中，從已知到未知、從有限到無限的必然發展過程。它將向我們展示，宗教並非某種外來的、強加於人的東西，而是人類意識中一種有機的、內在的成長。它將證明，即使是最粗糙的拜物教形式，也蘊含著對無限者的模糊感知；而最高級的一神教，則是這種感知在歷史中逐漸淨化與昇華的結果。宗教學並不評判這些宗教形式的優劣，而是將它們視為人類精神進化的必要階段。它承認，在每一種宗教中，都存在著真理的碎片，也存在著錯誤的陰影；但整體而言，人類的宗教史是一部從黑暗走向光明、從束縛走向解放的偉大史詩。這部史詩的終章，並非由任何單一的宗教所書寫，而是由人類對無限者的永恆追尋所共同譜寫。而宗教學的任務，便是解讀這部史詩的每一行經文，並指出那引領我們走向終極真理的、隱而不顯的線索。

以下是我們所追尋的漫長旅程的終點；在此，那無限者——曾如隔著面紗般顯現於山川、日月、蒼穹、無盡的黎明、天父、造物主毘首羯磨（Viśvakarman）、眾生之主生主（Prajāpati）之中——終於以印度智識所能達到的最高、最純粹的形式被看見。他們問道：我們能定義祂嗎？或能理解祂嗎？不，他們回答：我們所能說的，只是「不，不！」祂不是這個，祂不是那個；祂不是造物主，不是父親，不是蒼穹或太陽，不是河流或山巒。無論我們曾如何稱呼祂，祂都不是。我們無法理解或命名祂，但我們能感受祂；我們無法認識祂，但我們能領悟祂；而一旦我們找到了祂，便再也無法逃離祂。我們得享安息，我們獲得自由，我們蒙受祝福。他們耐心等待死亡來臨前的短短數年：他們不做任何事來延長衰老，但同時也認為親手結束自己的生命是錯誤的。他們已達到了在世上的永生，並堅信沒有任何新的生死能再次將他們與那無限者分開——儘管我們在其中看到了某種道（Logos）的概念。雖然梵（Brahman）最終意指宇宙的起因，並常與最高的阿特曼（Ātman）或自我（Self）等同，但其發展歷程與亞歷山大的道（Logos）不同；且無論如何，從歷史角度來看，這兩股思想潮流是完全不相干的。
1 Manu, VI, 45：「他不應渴求死亡，也不應渴求生命；他當靜候命定之時，如同僱工等待他的工資。」
他們所找到的、或已找到他們的永恆自我，達到了361。
然而，他們並不相信自我（Self）的徹底消滅。請回想那段對話：因陀羅（Indra）被描繪為耐心求取自我知識的角色。他先是在水中的倒影裡尋找自我；接著在作夢時的靈魂中尋找；然後在深眠狀態的靈魂中尋找。但即便如此，他仍不滿足，說道：「不，這不可能；因為這位沉睡者既不知道他自己（他的自我）就是『我』，也不知道任何存在的事物。他已陷入徹底的湮滅。我看不出這有何益處。」
但他的老師如何回答呢？「這個身體是會朽壞的，」他說，「並且始終被死亡所束縛，但它卻是自我（Self）的居所，而自我是不朽且無形體的。當自我具有形體時（當它認為這個身體就是我，而我就是這個身體），自我便被快樂與痛苦所束縛。只要它如此具有形體，便無法擺脫快樂與痛苦。但當自我脫離形體時（當它認識到自身不同於身體），那麼快樂與痛苦便再也無法觸及它。」
然而，這個自我（Self），即寧靜的靈魂，或至高者（the highest person），並不會消亡，它只是回歸自身；它甚至歡欣、大笑、嬉戲，但僅作為旁觀者，從不憶念其出生所依附的身體。祂是眼睛的自我（Self），眼睛本身不過是工具；那個知道「我將說此、我將聽此、我將思此」者，便是自我；舌頭、耳朵、心智都不過是工具。心智是祂神聖的眼睛，透過那神聖的眼睛，自我看見一切美好之物，並為之歡欣。
在此我們看到，滅絕（annihilation）確實並非……
